name: Prima della Scala — La Scala Opening Night
tagline: One December night when Milan dresses in black tie and the whole opera world holds its breath.
dates: December 7, 2026 — Verdi's Otello, conducted by Myung-Whun Chung (confirmed; run continues to January 5, 2027)
history: Teatro alla Scala opened on 3 August 1778 with Antonio Salieri's Europa riconosciuta, built to Giuseppe Piermarini's neoclassical design on the site of the burned-down Teatro Regio Ducale and named for the church of Santa Maria alla Scala that was demolished to make way for it. Within a few decades it had become the gravitational center of Italian opera: Rossini, Donizetti and Bellini premiered works here, and Verdi's relationship with the house — from Nabucco's triumphant 1842 premiere to the world premieres of Otello (1887) and Falstaff (1893) — made La Scala synonymous with Italian musical identity. Allied bombs gutted the auditorium in August 1943; its reopening concert on 11 May 1946, conducted by a returning Arturo Toscanini with the young Renata Tebaldi among the soloists, was celebrated as a symbol of Italy's postwar rebirth.

The tradition of opening the season on 7 December — the feast of Sant'Ambrogio, Milan's patron saint — was fixed in 1951, when music director Victor de Sabata inaugurated the season with Verdi's I vespri siciliani starring Maria Callas. Ever since, the 'Prima' has been Italy's most watched cultural ritual: the President of the Republic and heads of state in the royal box, paparazzi on the red carpet, and the fearsome loggionisti — the connoisseurs of the cheap upper gallery — ready to bless or bury a performance. Their power is real: in 2006 tenor Roberto Alagna walked off stage mid-Aida after being booed, an understudy finishing the act in jeans. The evening is broadcast live on Rai and, through the city's Prima Diffusa program, screened free in dozens of venues across Milan, from the Galleria Vittorio Emanuele to prisons and hospitals.

The 2026 Prima is a genuinely historic one: it inaugurates the first season of Myung-Whun Chung as La Scala's music director, with a new Otello staged by Damiano Michieletto — Verdi's Shakespearean masterpiece returning to the very house where it premiered in 1887. Brian Jagde sings the title role opposite Eleonora Buratto's Desdemona and Luca Salsi's Iago. The evening also marks fifty years since the first televised La Scala opening night on Rai, and it anchors a 2026/27 season of thirteen operas that runs deep into the traveler's Zürich year.
whyGo: This is opera's most storied single evening — a 250-year-old house, a new music director's debut season, and Verdi's Otello returning to the stage where it was born. Even without a golden ticket, being in Milan on Sant'Ambrogio is electric: free live screenings across the city, the Oh Bej! Oh Bej! Christmas fair, and a whole capital treating one opera as front-page news. From Zürich it is barely three hours away — the easiest world-class cultural pilgrimage on the calendar.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Milano Centrale (EuroCity direct, via Gotthard Base Tunnel)
gettingThere.notes: Several direct EC trains daily; book SBB or Trenitalia supersaver fares up to 3 months out. Centrale is a 10-min metro hop (M3 to Duomo/Montenapoleone) from the theatre.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Milan Malpensa (MXP) with SWISS, then Malpensa Express to Cadorna/Centrale
gettingThere.notes: Rarely faster than the train once airports are factored in; useful only if combining with onward travel. December fog can delay Malpensa arrivals.

gettingThere.cost: ~€60–90 (Swiss vignette CHF 40 if not held, Italian tolls ~€15, fuel)
gettingThere.notes: Not recommended: central Milan has the Area C congestion charge (€7.50/day), scarce expensive parking, and possible December snow on the Gotthard approach. Take the train.

costs.notes: The opening-night ticket is the whole game: gala seats historically run from roughly €150 in the gallery to €2,500–3,000 in the stalls and boxes, and sell out almost instantly. Thrifty assumes the free Prima Diffusa screening, supersaver trains and a budget hotel for 2 nights; midrange assumes a seat at a later Otello performance in the same run (€50–300) plus a 4-star hotel for 3 nights; splurge is a stalls/box Prima ticket, Grand Hotel et de Milan-class lodging and post-opera dinner. Hotels citywide spike December 5–8 for the Sant'Ambrogio/Immacolata holiday weekend.

booking.label: Teatro alla Scala — official site & box office
Prima Diffusa — free screenings across Milan (Edison)

weather: Early December in Milan is cold, grey and often foggy: highs around 6–8°C, lows near freezing, with damp Po Valley chill that cuts deeper than the numbers suggest. Pack a heavy overcoat, scarf and gloves — essential if you're standing at an outdoor Prima Diffusa screening — plus formal wear (dark suit or black tie) if you're going inside the theatre.
tips: Prima tickets sell out within minutes and top €3,000 — watch teatroallascala.org from September/October 2026 for the sale date, and treat any resale offer with suspicion. A far saner play: book a later performance of the exact same Otello production (Dec 10, 2026 – Jan 5, 2027) for €50–300.

Can't get in? Do the Prima Diffusa: 30+ free live screenings across Milan on Dec 7, including the big screen at the Ottagono of Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II, two minutes from the theatre. Arrive 1–2 hours early for the Galleria — seats are first-come.

The Prima starts at 18:00 sharp (it's broadcast live on Rai 1) and latecomers are not admitted until an interval. Dress code inside is serious: dark suit minimum, black tie common in the stalls and boxes.

Dec 7–8 is a double holiday (Sant'Ambrogio + Immacolata): book your hotel 3+ months out, and expect the Oh Bej! Oh Bej! Christmas market around Castello Sforzesco — a great daytime companion to the evening opera.

From Zürich, lock in EC train supersaver fares the day booking opens (~3 months ahead); the 3h17 ride through the Gotthard Base Tunnel beats flying door-to-door. An early-afternoon train on Dec 7 still makes an 18:00 curtain, but going Dec 6 is safer.

For regular (non-Prima) performances, La Scala releases ~140 cheap gallery tickets via an in-person queue system on performance day — register at the box office in the morning. Also visit the Museo Teatrale alla Scala (theatre museum) for a peek into the auditorium from a box, even without a performance ticket.

localRecs.name: Camparino in Galleria
localRecs.note: The 1915 Art Nouveau bar where Campari aperitivo culture was born — in the Galleria, 100 m from the theatre. Perfect pre-opera bitter and canapés.

localRecs.name: Savini
localRecs.note: The historic post-opera dinner institution in the Galleria since 1867 — Verdi, Puccini and Callas all ate here. Book ahead for after the Prima; risotto alla milanese is the move.

localRecs.name: Museo Teatrale alla Scala
localRecs.note: The theatre's own museum: Verdi manuscripts, Callas portraits, and daytime access to view the jewel-box auditorium from a box when rehearsals allow.

localRecs.name: Brera
localRecs.note: Milan's artiest quarter, a 10-minute walk from La Scala — Pinacoteca di Brera by day, candle-lit wine bars on Via Fiori Chiari after the curtain.

localRecs.name: Trattoria Masuelli San Marco
localRecs.note: Family-run since 1921 and beloved by locals for true Milanese cooking — ossobuco, cassoeula in winter, and a risotto worth the short taxi ride.

photos.caption: Piermarini's neoclassical facade of Teatro alla Scala on Piazza della Scala, Milan
The gilded horseshoe auditorium of La Scala — six tiers of boxes beneath the great chandelier
Inside the auditorium: red velvet boxes and the stage that premiered Verdi's Otello in 1887
